1: wayyēreḏ šimšôn timnātāh wayyarʾ ʾiššāh bətimnātāh mibənôt pəlištîm — 'Samson went down to Timnah and saw a woman in Timnah from the daughters of the Philistines.' yāraḏ (H3381, to go down) — Timnah (timnātāh H8553) lay in the Sorek Valley below the hill country of Dan, so 'going down' is geographically accurate. The woman is from the Philistines (pəlištîm H6430) — the dominant coastal people who oppressed Israel throughout Samson's era. Samson's entire career is conducted at the intersection of Israelite identity and Philistine territory; his marriages, riddles, and battles all occur on this borderland.
3: wayyōʾmer ləhî minnāh kî hîʾ yāšərāh bəʿênāy — 'Get her for me, for she is right in my eyes.' yāšar (H3474, to be straight/right/upright) + bəʿênāy (in my eyes) — the same phrase as Judges' refrain 'hayyāšār bəʿênāyw' (what is right in his own eyes, 17:6; 21:25). Samson's demand for this woman is the personal embodiment of the book's theological problem: Israel does what is right in its own eyes rather than in YHWH's eyes. His father objects on grounds of the ban against intermarriage (Deut 7:3); Samson overrides by asserting his own judgment. The narrator's aside in v4 — 'his father and mother did not know that it was from YHWH' — adds divine sovereignty: YHWH is working his purpose even through Samson's rebellious desire.
4: wəʾāḇîw wəʾimmô lōʾ yāḏəʿû kî mēYHWH hîʾ kî-ṯōʾănāh hûʾ məḇaqquēš miPəlištîm — 'His father and mother did not know that it was from YHWH, for he was seeking an occasion against the Philistines.' tōʾănāh (H8385, occasion/pretext/opportunity — from ʾānaʾ, to seek a pretext, only here) is a technical term for the spark needed to start a conflict. YHWH is sovereignly using Samson's personal desires to create the necessary confrontation with Philistia. The verse is a remarkable statement of divine providence operating through human passions — YHWH does not approve of the intermarriage, yet works through it to accomplish his deliverance purpose.
6: wattiṣlaḥ ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH wayyəšassəʿēhû kəšassaʿ haggerî wəʾumāh ʾên bəyāḏô — 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he tore it apart as one tears apart a young goat, and he had nothing in his hand.' ṣālaḥ (H6743, to rush/advance upon — used consistently for the Spirit's empowerment of the judges: 3:10 Othniel, 11:29 Jephthah, 14:19 Samson again; and 1 Sam 10:6,10; 16:13 for Saul and David) — the Spirit's arrival is sudden, powerful, and directive. šissēaʿ (H8156, to split/tear — the verb used for tearing apart a kid) applied to a lion carcass: Samson tears a lion bare-handed as though it were a kid. The 'nothing in his hand' detail is significant: this is pure Spirit-empowered strength, not a weapon feat.
8: wayyelek ləqaḥtāh wayyāsar lirʾôt ʾēt mappelet hāʾaryēh wəhinnēh ʿăḏat dəḇôrîm bəgəwiyyat hāʾaryēh ûḏəḇāš — 'He turned aside to see the carcass of the lion, and behold, there was a swarm of bees in the carcass of the lion, and honey.' mappelet (H4658, carcass/fallen thing — from nāpal to fall) — the lion's body. That bees have colonized it implies it has dried out (bees avoid rotting flesh). The honey in the lion's carcass is technically from a ṭāmēʾ (H2931, unclean) animal (Lev 11:27-28 — animals with paws are unclean; contact with their carcasses renders one unclean). Samson eats the honey without hesitation, compounding his Nazirite violations (no contact with corpses, Num 6:6). The honey becomes his riddle's answer: sweet from the strong.
12: wayyōʾmer lāhem šimšôn ʾāḥûḏāh-nāʾ lākem ḥîḏāh ʾim-hāḡēḏ taggîḏû ʾōtāh lî šiḇʿat yəmê hammišteh — 'Samson said to them: Let me now put a riddle to you. If you can explain it to me within the seven days of the feast...' ḥîḏāh (H2420, riddle/enigma/dark saying — from ḥûḏ H2330, to tie knots) is a wisdom genre: a knotted/twisted saying that conceals meaning. Riddles were a form of wisdom contest at banquets (1 Kgs 10:1 — Queen of Sheba tested Solomon with ḥîḏôt). Samson's riddle is personal-autobiographical: only he and YHWH know the experience behind it, making it technically unsolvable from public knowledge.
14: min-hāʾōkēl yāṣāʾ maʾăkāl ûmēʿaz yāṣāʾ māṯôq — 'Out of the eater came something to eat, and out of the strong came something sweet.' ʾōkēl (H398 participle, the eater — a lion) / maʾăkāl (H3978, food/something to eat — honey) / ʿaz (H5794, strong/fierce — the lion) / māṯôq (H4966, sweet — honey). The riddle is a verbal paradox built on unexpected reversals: the destroyer produces food; the fierce produces sweetness. The answer requires knowing the specific lion-and-honey experience. Without that knowledge, the Philistines can only solve it by extracting it from Samson's wife — which is exactly what they do, triggering the conflict YHWH intended (v4).
19: wattiṣlaḥ ʿālāyw rûaḥ YHWH wayyēreḏ ʾašqəlôn wayyak mēhem šəlōšîm ʾîš — 'The Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon and struck thirty men of them.' The Spirit's empowerment here enables Samson's 'payment' of the wager debt — thirty sets of garments taken from thirty Philistine dead. This is the Spirit being used for what looks like personal vengeance, but the narrative frames it as YHWH's larger campaign against Philistia. Ashkelon (ʾašqəlôn H831) was one of the five major Philistine cities (Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Gath, Ekron), demonstrating Samson's raids span the entire Philistine territory.
2: wayyaʿal wayyaggēḏ ləʾāḇîw ûləʾimmô wayyōʾmer ʾiššāh rāʾîtî lətimnātāh mibənôt pəlištîm wəʿattāh qəḥû-ʾōtāh lî ləʾiššāh — 'He came up and told his father and his mother: I saw a woman in Timnah, from the daughters of the Philistines. Now get her for me as a wife.' rāʾîtî (H7200, I saw) — sight triggers desire throughout Samson's story: he sees the Timnite woman (14:1), the Gaza prostitute (16:1), and Delilah (16:4). The pattern of desire-from-sight leading to disaster mirrors the Eden narrative (Gen 3:6) and foreshadows his literal blindness in 16:21 — Samson who lived by sight ends without eyes. qəḥû-ʾōtāh lî (get her for me) — the imperative to parents; he makes demands rather than requests, a pattern of self-will throughout.
5: wayyēreḏ šimšôn wəʾāḇîw wəʾimmô timnātāh wayyāḇōʾû ʾad-karmê timnāh wəhinnēh kəpîr ʾărāyôt šōʾēg liqrāʾtô — 'Samson went down with his father and mother to Timnah; when he came to the vineyards of Timnah, behold, a young lion came roaring toward him.' kəpîr ʾărāyôt (H3715 H738, young lion — a lion past the cub stage, in full strength) — the Timnah vineyards are themselves ironic setting: Samson the Nazirite (nāzîr, consecrated to abstain from grapes, Num 6:4) walks through a vineyard on his way to compound his violations. The lion ambush sets up the honey riddle and triggers the Spirit's first public display through Samson.
7: wayyēreḏ wayyədabbēr lāʾiššāh wattiîšar bəʿênê šimšôn — 'He went down and spoke with the woman, and she seemed right in Samson's eyes.' wattiîšar (H3474, she seemed right/good/pleasing) — the narrator repeats the root yāšar from v3: the woman who 'is right in my eyes' (v3) now 'seems right in his eyes' upon closer acquaintance. The repetition reinforces that Samson's evaluation is purely subjective and self-referential. He never learns her name; she exists in the narrative only in relation to him — a troubling pattern in the Samson cycle that reflects his inability to form covenantal relationships.
9: wayyirḏēhû ʾel-kappāyw wayyēlek hālôk wəʾākōl wayyēlek ʾel-ʾāḇîw wəʾel-ʾimmô wayyittēn lāhem wayyōʾkelû wəlōʾ higgîḏ lāhem kî mippegerhat hāʾaryēh rāḏāh hadəḇāš — 'He scraped the honey into his hands and went on, eating as he went. He came to his father and mother and gave some to them, and they ate. But he did not tell them that he had scraped the honey from the carcass of the lion.' rādāh (H7287/H7300, to scrape/draw out — the specific act of extracting honey from a comb) — Samson knows that touching the lion's carcass renders him and anyone who touches his food ritually unclean (Num 6:6-9). He conceals this from his parents, making them unknowing participants in defilement. The concealment is not caution but complicity: the Nazirite violates his vow and involves his family.
10: wayyēreḏ ʾāḇîw ʾel-hāʾiššāh wayyaʿaś šam šimšôn mišteh kî kēn yaʿăśû habbaḥûrîm — 'His father went down to the woman; Samson made a feast there, for so the young men used to do.' mišteh (H4960, feast/drinking feast — from šātāh H8354, to drink) — the feast was specifically a wine-drinking celebration, the standard wedding feast. That Samson the Nazirite makes a mišteh — a wine feast — is a direct Nazirite violation (Num 6:3: no wine or strong drink, no grape juice, no fresh or dried grapes). The narrator notes it was customary for young men, explaining why no one objects, but the violation stands: the consecrated man participates in what his vow forbids.
11: wayhî kirʾôtām ʾōtô wayyiqḥû šəlōšîm mēraʿîm wayyihyû ʾittô — 'When they saw him, they brought thirty companions to be with him.' mēraʿîm (H4828 plural, companions/attendants) — the thirty companions assigned by the Philistines may have been social custom or security measure. In the context of Samson's isolation (his parents returned home) and the Philistine setting, thirty men watching him functions as surveillance. The number thirty becomes the wager amount: 30 linen garments and 30 festal garments (v12-13) — if Samson loses the riddle, these thirty men profit. If he wins, they must pay. Their subsequent extortion of the answer through his wife reveals their intent from the start.
13: wəʾim-lōʾ tûkəlû ləhaggîḏ lî wənatatttem ʾattem lî šəlōšîm sādîn wəšəlōšîm ḥălipôt bəḡāḏîm — 'But if you cannot tell me, then you shall give me thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothes.' sādîn (H5466, linen garment/sheet — fine linen undergarment) and ḥălipôt bəḡāḏîm (H2487 H899, changes of clothing — festal outer garments) — the wager involves two complete sets of clothing for thirty men each. The total value is substantial: 60 quality garments. Samson is wagering on the unprovability of a private experience; the Philistines are gambling that social pressure on his wife will extract the answer — which is exactly what they proceed to do.
15: wayhî bayyôm haššəḇîʿî wayyōʾmərû ləʾēšet šimšôn pittî ʾet-ʾîšēk wəyaggēḏ-lānû ʾet-haḥîḏāh — 'On the seventh day they said to Samson's wife: Entice your husband, that he may tell us the riddle.' pittāh (H6601, to entice/seduce/deceive — the same verb used of false prophets in Ezek 14:9 and the seduction of a virgin in Exod 22:16) — the verb is strong: they are asking the woman to use seduction and manipulation to extract intelligence. pen-niśrəpāh ʾôtāk wə-ʾet-bêt ʾāḇîk bāʾēš — 'lest we burn you and your father's house with fire' — the threat is lethal and immediate; the woman is trapped between her husband and her people's threats.
16: wattibekkeh ʾēšet šimšôn ʿālāyw wattoʾmer śānēʾtanî wəlōʾ ʾăhaḇtānî — 'Samson's wife wept over him and said: You only hate me; you do not love me.' śānēʾ (H8130, to hate — the antonym of covenant love ʾāhab) and ʾāhab (H157, to love) — the wife frames the riddle secret as a test of marital love. Her accusation reframes an intelligence extraction as an emotional appeal: if you loved me, you'd tell me. haḥîḏāh ḥaḏtāh wəlōʾ higgadtāh lî — 'you have put a riddle to my countrymen and have not told me.' The irony is that she is being coerced by her kinsmen to extract what she frames as a matter of love — her tears and accusations work on Samson's affections exactly as the Philistines calculated.
17: wattibekkeh ʿālāyw šiḇʿat hayyāmîm ʾăšer-hāyāh lāhem hammišteh wayhî bayyôm haššəḇîʿî wayyaggēḏ-lāh kî-hēṣîqat-lô — 'She wept before him the seven days that their feast lasted, and on the seventh day he told her because she pressed him hard.' hēṣîq (H6693, to press hard/distress — same root as 16:16 where Delilah presses Samson daily until his soul was shortened to death) — the verb anticipates the Delilah pattern: a woman's persistent emotional pressure extracts Samson's secret. He is vulnerable to relational pressure from women in a way he is not vulnerable to physical force. The seven-day persistence is successful: love conquers the riddle's security.
18: wayyōʾmərû lô ʾanšê hāʿîr bayyôm haššəḇîʿî bəṭerem yiššaqep haḥammāh mah-māṯôq midəḇāš ûmah-ʿaz mēʾaryēh — 'The men of the city said to him on the seventh day before the sun went down: What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?' Samson's devastating response: lûlēʾ ḥărašttem bəʿeglātî lōʾ məṣāʾtem ḥîḏātî — 'If you had not plowed with my heifer, you would not have found out my riddle.' ḥāraš (H2790, to plow/engrave) + ʿeglāh (H5697, heifer) — the metaphor is agricultural and sexual: 'plowing with my heifer' = using my wife for your purposes. The image of a wife as a working animal is harsh and reflects Samson's objectifying perspective; yet it accurately captures what the Philistines did — they used her as a tool to extract information.
20: wattihyeh ʾēšet šimšôn ləmērēʿēhû ʾăšer rēʿāh-lô — 'And Samson's wife was given to his companion, who had been his best man.' mērēʿ (H4828, companion/friend — the companion who served as his best man at the wedding, called his mērēʿ) — the wife is transferred to the wedding companion when Samson abandons her in anger. This completes the Timnah episode's arc: Samson went down seeking a wife and returns without one. The transfer becomes the pretext for the fox-and-fire campaign (15:1-5). Every action in the Samson cycle sets up the next escalation; nothing is resolved cleanly because Samson acts from anger rather than covenant faithfulness.
